nativeRange.habitat: Coral and rubble areas on steep outer reef slopes, recorded at depths of about 8–70 m; usually solitary or in small groups of four to five individuals

diet.notes: Generally accepts a varied diet of enriched frozen brine and mysis shrimp alongside spirulina, marine algae, and a quality prepared marine angelfish formula. Feeding 2–3 times per day is commonly advised to help discourage nipping at corals and clam mantles.

summary: A small, prized dwarf angelfish endemic to Christmas and Cocos (Keeling) Islands, with bright yellow head and shoulders fading to deep blue posteriorly. Generally semi-aggressive and best kept singly.
careNotes: The Joculator Angelfish is a deepwater Indian Ocean dwarf angel endemic to Christmas and Cocos (Keeling) Islands, which contributes to its scarcity and price in the trade. It is generally considered hardy once established, but should be the last fish introduced to a peaceful community and provided with ample live rock for grazing and shelter alongside open swimming space. Sources widely report it as intolerant of conspecifics and aggressive toward similar-shaped Centropyge or large angelfishes such as the Bicolor and Rock Beauty, so it is best kept singly except in very large systems where a male/female pair can be introduced simultaneously. Reef compatibility is reported with caution — it has been known to nip stony coral polyps, soft coral, and clam mantles, with frequent feeding generally helping to reduce this. Source disagreement on adult size: LiveAquaria lists ~4.5 in (≈11 cm) maximum, Quality Marine cites 3.5 in (≈9 cm), and FishBase records 9 cm TL — a captive-attainable range of roughly 8–11 cm has been used here. Source disagreement on temperature: LiveAquaria recommends 72–78°F (22–25.6°C) for aquarium care while FishBase reports a wild thermal preference of 27.9–28.6°C; the aquarium-typical 22–26°C range has been retained as it aligns with related Centropyge husbandry. Handle with care as angelfishes have sharp cheek (preopercular) spines that can tangle in nets.
breedingNotes: Centropyge joculator is a protogynous hermaphrodite and forms harems consisting of a single dominant male with up to four females; if the male is lost the dominant female generally transitions to male. Captive breeding has been reported only sporadically by specialist breeders and is considered very difficult at hobbyist level; like other dwarf angels it is a dusk pelagic broadcast spawner with no parental care.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked scientific name, family/order, native range (Cocos and Christmas Islands), max size (9.0 cm TL), and wild thermal preference (27.9–28.6°C — warmer than the LiveAquaria aquarium range of 22–25.6°C; aquarium-typical 22–26°C used).

additional.name: Quality Marine
additional.notes: Cross-checked tank size (70 gal / ~265 L → rounded up to 300 L per house style), pH (8.1–8.4), salinity (1.020–1.025), kH (8–12 dKH), conspecific aggression toward same-shape angelfish, and reef caution.

additional.name: Wikipedia
additional.notes: Used to confirm protogynous hermaphroditism and haremic social structure (one male with up to four females).

fish.breedingNotes: Protogynous hermaphrodite and haremic dusk pelagic spawner. Captive rearing of larvae has been reported only by specialist breeders; not commercially bred at hobbyist level.
